7: echomen de ton thesauron touton en ostrakinois skeuesin (echomen de ton thēsauron touton en ostrakinois skeuesin): 'we have this treasure in clay jars.' Ostrakinos (earthen/clay) is the material of the most common, cheapest, most breakable pottery — cheap oil lamps, storage vessels, disposable containers. The treasure (the gospel of the glory of God in Christ) is disproportionately housed in the most fragile containers possible. The purpose clause: 'to show that the surpassing power belongs to God and not to us.' Paul's paradox of ministerial weakness inverts all Greco-Roman honor-shame expectations about what a legitimate messenger of the gods should look like.
1: Dia touto, echontes ten diakonian tauten, kathos eleethemen, ouk ekkakomen: having this ministry through mercy (eleeo — the compassion verb), we do not lose heart (ekkakeo — to be weary, give in to evil). The mercy-basis: the ministry was received as a mercy-gift, not earned — therefore hardships do not invalidate it. Endurance is grounded in grace, not personal resilience.
2: Alla apeipametha ta krypta tes aischynes: renounced the hidden things of shame (aischyne). What Paul rejects: cunning (panourgia — unprincipled all-working cleverness), distorting (dolountes — adulterating) the word of God. Alla te phanerosi tes aletheias: by the open display of truth (aletheia). The apostolic word is transparent — nothing hidden, no manipulation of meaning.
3: Ei de kai estin kekalymmenon to euangelion hemon: if the gospel is veiled. The veil of 2 Corinthians 3 reappears applied to the gospel. En tois apollymenois estin kekalymmenon: it is veiled among those who are perishing (apollymi) — the covering is on the hearers, not in the message. The gospel itself is not unclear or insufficient.
4: En hois ho theos tou aionos toutou: the god of this age (aion, dispute level 2 — the present temporal age). MKT 'age' — precise: the adversary is the ruling power of the period between Christ's comings, not 'the world' spatially. Etyphlose ta noemata ton apiston: blinded the minds/thoughts. Eis to me augasai ton photismon tou euangeliou tes doxes tou Christou: so they cannot see the gospel's light of Christ's glory. The adversary's strategy is epistemic blindness, not direct power.
5: Ou gar heautous keryssomen alla Iesoun Christon Kyrion: for we do not preach ourselves but Jesus Christ as Lord. The not-ourselves/Christ-as-Lord contrast is the anti-boasting principle applied to preaching. Heautous de doulous hymon dia Iesoun: ourselves as slaves (douloi) of you through Jesus — the apostle is slave of the congregation on account of Jesus.
6: Hoti ho theos ho eipon ek skotous phos lampsei: the God who said 'out of darkness light shall shine' — Genesis 1:3 applied to the new creation. Hos elampsen en tais kardiais hemon pros photismon tes gnoseos tes doxes tou theou en prosopo Christou: who shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of God's glory in the face of Christ. Creation light-event replicated in conversion: God's creative word lights the inner darkness. 'In the face of Christ' — the face where God's glory is now visible, replacing the veiled face of Moses.
8: En panti thlibomenoi all' ou stenochoroymenoi: pressed (thlibo) on every side but not crushed/hemmed in (stenochoreo). The peristasis catalogue (2 Corinthians 4:8-9): four pairs of contradictions, each with a negative and a limit. Structure is deliberate — real danger but the danger does not define the final word. Aporoymenoi all' ouk exaporoymenoi: at a loss (aporeo) but not utterly at a loss — the intensified prefix ex- marks the absolute limit not reached.
9: Diokomenoi all' ouk enkataleipoumenoi: persecuted but not forsaken (egkataleipo — the verb of Psalms 22:1, the dereliction cry). Divine non-abandonment stands even in persecution. Kataballomenoi all' ouk apollymenoi: struck down but not destroyed (apollymi — same verb as 'perishing' in 2 Corinthians 4:3). Paul is in the same category of danger as those spiritually perishing, but the outcome differs because of Christ.
10: Pantote ten nekrosin tou Iesou en to somati peripherontes: always carrying in the body the dying (nekrosis) of Jesus — specifically Jesus' death-pattern, not just mortality in general. Hina kai he zoe tou Iesou en to somati hemon phaneroth e: so that Jesus' life may be manifested in our body. The Christological logic of display: the community sees the living Christ through Paul's endurance of death.
11: Aei gar hemeis hoi zontes eis thanaton paradidometha dia Iesoun: we who are living are always being handed over (paradidomi — the betrayal/delivery verb, used for Jesus' delivery to death) for Jesus' sake. Sarx (dispute level 2) here: the physical, mortal body — the vulnerable mortality of the human frame, not the sinful principle. The life of Jesus manifested in mortal flesh is the apostolic witness.
12: Hoste ho thanatos en hemin energeitai, he de zoe en hymin: death is working (energeo) in us, life in you. The transfer-logic: Paul absorbs the dying so the community receives the life. Peristasis-suffering is not random hardship but apostolic service — the carrying of death enables the community's experience of life.
13: Echontes de to auto pneuma tes pisteos kata to gegrammenon: having the same spirit of faith according to what is written — Psalms 116:10 (LXX): 'I believed, therefore I spoke.' Pistis (dispute level 2): Paul cites the Psalmist's speech-from-faith as his model. Faith in the resurrection-God produces speech — the ground of the continuation of apostolic proclamation in the face of death.
14: Eidos hoti ho egeias ton Kyrion Iesoun kai hemas syn Iesou egerei: knowing that the one who raised the Lord Jesus will also raise us with Jesus. Kai parasteisei syn hymin: and will present us together with you. The resurrection hope encompasses both Paul and community — raised and presented together. The syn-prefix: resurrection is communal, not solitary.
15: Ta gar panta di' hymas: all of this is for your sake. Hina he charis pleonousa dia ton pleionon ten eucharistian perisseuse eis ten doxan tou theou: so that grace (charis, dispute level 2), as it spreads, may increase thanksgiving. Eucharistia overflowing to God's glory is the telos of apostolic mission.
16: Dio ouk ekkakomen: therefore we do not lose heart — the refrain of 4:1 repeated, forming an inclusio around the peristasis catalogue. Ho exo hemon anthropos diaphtheitai: the outer person is wasting away (diaphtheiro). Ho eso hemon anakainoytai hemera kai hemera: the inner person is being renewed day by day — progressive outward decay matched by progressive inward renewal.
17: To gar parautika elaphron tes thlipseos hemon kath' hyperbolen eis hyperbolen aionion baros doxes katergazetai hemin: the momentary lightness of affliction (elaphron — same root as 'fickle' in 2 Corinthians 1:17, here ironic) is working (katergazomai) an eternal weight of glory beyond all comparison. Aionios (dispute level 2 — eternal/everlasting): the eschatological duration of glory contrasts with the temporal affliction. Light-and-momentary vs heavy-and-eternal reversal.
18: Me skopoynton hemon ta blepomena alla ta me blepomena: not attending to (skopeo — scoping, fixing attention on) the visible things but the invisible. Ta gar blepomena proskaira, ta de me blepomena aionios: visible things are temporary (proskairos — seasonal, for a time); invisible things eternal (aionios). The epistemological reorientation grounds apostolic endurance in a different mode of perception — faith that sees the eternal behind the temporal.
